Performing manipulation of the argument health_url results in server-side request forgery. The attack can be initiated remotely. The exploit is now public and may be used. The patch is named 0ff771dc1933d5a6b78f804115e78a7d8625c3f3. To fix this issue, it is recommended to deploy a patch. The vendor responded with a vulnerability confirmat
cves.how_introduced: The application allowed users to provide arbitrary URLs for content processing without validation, enabling the server to be coerced into making requests to internal or external resources.

bug_commits.author: Wenjie Zhang
bug_commits.message: refactor(database): 重构知识库信息页面组件化实现

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the 'URL' upload mode and the associated 'urlList' input field in the FileUploadModal component, which was the entry point for the SSRF vulnerability. The fix commit subsequently removed this functionality entirely, confirming that the initial implementation of this feature was the source of the insecure pattern.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application allowed users to provide arbitrary URLs for content processing without validation, enabling the server to be coerced into making requests to internal or external resources.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: processing user-supplied URLs via process_url_to_markdown without restriction or validation
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added the 'urlList' input and the logic to process user-supplied URLs, which lacked server-side validation, allowing the application to be coerced into making arbitrary requests.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix removes and disables the URL-upload path in FileUploadModal.vue, indicating that the vulnerable behavior was the user-facing ability to submit arbitrary URLs for server-side fetching. The suspected commit is exactly the commit that introduced FileUploadModal.vue with the '输入网址' mode, the urlList input, and the code path that passes user-supplied URLs to store.addFiles({ items: urls, contentType: 'url', ... }). That feature is the frontend entry point for the SSRF condition later fixed by disabling URL uploads entirely.
evidence: Fix commit 0ff771dc removes the URL input UI, disables uploadMode selection for 'url', and deletes the chunkData branch that submitted urlList URLs to the backend.
Commit 40c2922132 introduces FileUploadModal.vue from scratch, including uploadMode='url', urlList, and URL submission logic in chunkData.
The same commit's store.addFiles supports contentType: 'url', forwarding user-controlled URL items to documentApi.addDocuments for server-side processing.
The CVE is SSRF via user-controlled URL/health_url processing; this commit introduced the exposed URL ingestion feature that enabled attackers to supply arbitrary remote targets.

cves.description: A vulnerability was found in PX4 PX4-Autopilot up to 1.16.0. Affected by this issue is the function MavlinkLogHandler::state_listing/MavlinkLogHandler::log_entry_from_id of the file src/modules/mavlink/mavlink_log_handler.cpp. The manipulation results in stack-based buffer overflow. The attack is only possible with local access. The patch is identified as 338595edd1d235efd885fd5e9f45e7f9dcf4013d. It is best practice to apply a patch to resolve this issue.

bug_commits.author: copilot-swe-agent[bot]
bug_commits.message: Fix stack buffer overflow in mavlink_log_handler sscanf calls

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the vulnerable pattern by changing the sscanf format string to %1023s in state_listing, which exceeds the size of the destination buffer (PX4_MAX_FILEPATH). This created a stack-based buffer overflow when reading log file paths that exceed the buffer capacity.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit replaced an unbounded %s with %1023s in state_listing, which is larger than the stack-allocated filepath buffer, allowing an attacker to overwrite adjacent stack memory when parsing malicious log entries.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected bug-introducing commit (BIC) did not introduce the buffer overflow vulnerability. Instead, it attempted to fix an existing unbounded buffer overflow (`%s`) by adding length limits (`%1023s` and `%255s`). Because the limit `1023` was still larger than the destination buffer in some environments, the vulnerability persisted and required a subsequent fix. Therefore, the BIC merely modified an existing vulnerability rather than introducing it.
evidence: The BIC changed an unbounded `%s` format string to `%1023s` and `%255s`.
The vulnerability is a stack-based buffer overflow in the same `sscanf` call.
Before the BIC, the code used `%s`, which is inherently vulnerable to buffer overflows.
The BIC attempted to fix the vulnerability but used a bound (`1023`) that was still larger than the buffer size (`PX4_MAX_FILEPATH`), leaving it partially vulnerable.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected BIC did modify the vulnerable sscanf in state_listing(), but it changed an already-unsafe unbounded "%s" into "%1023s" rather than introducing overflow where none existed before. Since filepath there is only PX4_MAX_FILEPATH bytes, the overflow condition pre-existed the BIC and remained present because 1023 can still exceed the actual buffer. In contrast, the same commit made log_entry_from_id() safer by changing "%s" to "%255s" for the 256-byte LogEntry.filepath buffer. Thus the commit touched relevant code but did not actually introduce the vulnerability; it only failed to fully fix an existing one in state_listing().
evidence: BIC diff shows state_listing() changed sscanf(..., "%s", ..., filepath) to sscanf(..., "%1023s", ..., filepath), so the unsafe read existed before the BIC.

At the BIC, state_listing() still uses char filepath[PX4_MAX_FILEPATH], while the later fix changed the format to %255s and added static_assert(PX4_MAX_FILEPATH >= 256), confirming 1023 was too large for that buffer.

BIC changed log_entry_from_id() from "%s" to "%255s" for entry->filepath, and the header at the BIC shows LogEntry.filepath is char filepath[256], meaning that path was fixed rather than introduced by the BIC.
The fix commit 8e0caba only adjusts state_listing() from %1023s to %255s, consistent with correcting an incomplete prior fix rather than removing a newly introduced flaw.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC (338595e) introduced the specific `%1023s` format specifier in `state_listing()` that the fix commit (8e0caba) later corrected to `%255s`. On NuttX platforms where `CONFIG_PATH_MAX` can be less than 1024, the `%1023s` specifier allows writing up to 1023 bytes into a `filepath[PX4_MAX_FILEPATH]` buffer, causing a stack-based buffer overflow. While the pre-BIC code had an even worse unbounded `%s`, the BIC introduced the specific vulnerable pattern (`%1023s`) that the CVE fix addresses. The CVE description explicitly identifies this commit (338595e) as the patch, but it was an incomplete fix that still left the `state_listing()` function vulnerable.
evidence: BIC changed `%s` to `%1023s` in state_listing() where buffer is `char filepath[PX4_MAX_FILEPATH]`
On NuttX, PX4_MAX_FILEPATH = CONFIG_PATH_MAX which can be less than 1024, making %1023s overflow the buffer
Fix commit changed exactly `%1023s` to `%255s` in state_listing() and added static_assert(PX4_MAX_FILEPATH >= 256)
The BIC properly fixed log_entry_from_id() with %255s but used an unsafe %1023s for state_listing()
CVE description explicitly names state_listing and identifies 338595e as the patch commit, indicating it was an incomplete fix

bug_commits.author: copilot-swe-agent[bot]
bug_commits.message: Use LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH macro for format specifier

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the macro LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH and replaced the hardcoded '255' width specifier in sscanf with this macro. By decoupling the width from the hardcoded value without enforcing a compile-time check against the actual buffer size, it created a maintenance hazard where the buffer size could be changed elsewhere without updating the macro, leading to the buffer overflow.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit introduced a macro-based width specifier for sscanf that was not programmatically linked to the buffer size constant. This allowed the buffer size to be modified in future commits without updating the macro, causing the sscanf width to exceed the actual buffer capacity and resulting in a stack-based buffer overflow.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced a new maintainability macro `LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH` hardcoded to "255" and replaced the previously inline `%255s` limits in both `sscanf` calls with that macro. At that commit, `LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE` was 256, so behavior was safe then, but the fix commit shows the actual vulnerability was the missing compile-time synchronization between the macro and `LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE`; it added a `static_assert(MavlinkLogHandler::LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE == 256, ...)` to prevent the width from becoming stale. Since the vulnerable pattern later fixed is precisely the introduction of a decoupled hardcoded width constant without enforcement, the BIC is the commit that introduced it.
evidence: BIC defines `#define LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH "255"` with no check tying it to `LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE`.
BIC changes both parsing sites to use `%" LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH "s` instead of literal `%255s`, creating an independent hardcoded constant.
At the same commit, `LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE` in `mavlink_log_handler.h` is `256`, so safety depends on that value never changing.

The fix commit adds only `static_assert(MavlinkLogHandler::LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE == 256, "Update LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH if LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE changes")`, confirming the flaw was lack of synchronization introduced by the macro change.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected BIC did not introduce the vulnerability. The vulnerability was caused by the use of `%s` without a width specifier in `sscanf`, which allowed stack-based buffer overflows. The suspected BIC is actually a later refactoring commit that replaced the hardcoded `255` width specifier (which was introduced in the actual fix) with a macro `LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH` to improve maintainability. Therefore, it is completely unrelated to introducing the vulnerability.
evidence: The vulnerability was a stack-based buffer overflow caused by using `%s` without a width specifier in `sscanf`.
The actual fix commit `338595edd1d235efd885fd5e9f45e7f9dcf4013d` replaced `%s` with `%1023s` and `%255s`.
The suspected BIC `ca336fb2995b2ea8549b9c9431c13a5bc1ad8ecb` merely refactored the hardcoded `255` width specifier into a macro `LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH`.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC is a pure refactoring that replaced hardcoded `%255s` in sscanf format strings with a macro `LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH` defined as `"255"`, producing identical behavior. Before the BIC, the code already had `%255s` with a 256-byte buffer - correctly synchronized. The BIC did not introduce any buffer overflow. The fix commit only adds a `static_assert` to guard against future desynchronization between the macro and `LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE`, which is a defensive improvement rather than a fix for an existing vulnerability. The BIC introduced the macro that could theoretically become desynchronized in the future, but it did not introduce an actual exploitable vulnerability.
evidence: Before BIC: sscanf used hardcoded '%255s' with LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE=256 buffer - correctly matched
BIC replaced '%255s' with macro LOG_FILEPATH_SCANF_WIDTH='255' - identical runtime behavior, no vulnerability introduced
Fix only adds static_assert(LOG_FILEPATH_SIZE == 256) to prevent future desynchronization - not fixing an existing overflow
The sscanf width specifier (255) correctly prevents buffer overflow for the 256-byte filepath buffer both before and after the BIC

cves.description: aws-mcp-server MCP server is vulnerable to command injection. An attacker can craft a prompt that once accessed by the MCP client will run arbitrary commands on the host system.
cves.how_introduced: The application failed to sufficiently sanitize or restrict user-provided input before passing it to the system shell, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands.

bug_commits.author: Alexei Ledenev (aider)
bug_commits.message: refactor: Enhance AWS MCP server with rate limiting, auth error detection, and command validation

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `validate_aws_command` function, which contains the logic for sanitizing user-provided AWS CLI commands. The vulnerability exists because this validation logic relies on `shlex.split` and basic string prefix checks, which are insufficient to prevent command injection when the input is passed to a shell-based execution context.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to sufficiently sanitize or restrict user-provided input before passing it to the system shell, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: executing user-supplied input as part of an 'aws' CLI command without strict validation or allowlisting
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented the `validate_aws_command` function using inadequate validation patterns (simple prefix checks and `shlex.split`), which failed to account for shell metacharacters or complex command chaining, thereby enabling command injection when the application later executed these commands via `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell`.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the first one that introduces the vulnerable execution pattern in the blamed file: it added `validate_aws_command()` that only checks the command starts with `aws`, while `execute_aws_command()` runs the full user-controlled string via `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(command)`. That combination enables shell metacharacter injection such as `aws ...; <arbitrary command>`, matching the CVE. The later fix replaces shell-based execution with `create_subprocess_exec` and moves to stronger security validation, so the vulnerability stems from this commit’s introduction of shell execution plus insufficient validation.
evidence: In commit 23c717b627ea5b0e0e71420c360bf0dfb85f4365, `src/aws_mcp_server/utils/cli_executor.py` executes user input with `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(command, ...)`.
The same commit adds `validate_aws_command()` that only enforces the command begins with `aws` and a minimal denylist; it does not reject shell metacharacters or parse-and-exec argv safely.
The fix commit’s code uses `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(*cmd_parts, ...)` in `src/aws_mcp_server/cli_executor.py`, indicating the security fix is to avoid shell interpretation.
The fix also moves validation into `aws_mcp_server.security`, including stricter pipe-command validation, showing prior validation was inadequate for preventing injection.

cves.description: MCP Python SDK has Unhandled Exception in Streamable HTTP Transport ,Leading to Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: The application fails to properly handle exceptions during streamable HTTP transport, allowing unhandled errors to crash the process.

bug_commits.author: David Soria Parra
bug_commits.message: Fix #201: Move incoming message stream from BaseSession to ServerSession (#325)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit replaced the internal stream-based message handling with direct calls to `self._handle_incoming(message)`. By removing the intermediate stream processing, it bypassed the existing error handling logic and introduced the vulnerability where unhandled exceptions in the transport layer propagate directly, causing the process to crash.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application fails to properly handle exceptions during streamable HTTP transport, allowing unhandled errors to crash the process.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: lack of try-except blocks around stream processing or network I/O operations
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit refactored the message processing loop to call `_handle_incoming` directly instead of sending messages through a memory stream, removing the implicit safety of the stream abstraction and failing to wrap the new direct calls in necessary try-except blocks.

bug_commits.pr_title: Fix #201: Move incoming message stream from BaseSession to ServerSession

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC rewired BaseSession._receive_loop to call subclass _handle_incoming() directly instead of enqueueing incoming items onto a separate memory stream. In ClientSession, _handle_incoming immediately awaits the user-supplied message_handler, so any exception from malformed stream input, validation, or callback handling now propagates out of _receive_loop and can terminate the task group. The fix later added broad exception handling around the entire receive loop specifically to prevent these unhandled exceptions from crashing the server/transport, which is the CVE’s core DoS condition.
evidence: BIC 568cbd1 changed _receive_loop from sending Exception/request/notification items to _incoming_message_stream_writer to directly awaiting self._handle_incoming(...) in BaseSession.
At commit 568cbd1, ClientSession._handle_incoming simply does 'await self._message_handler(req)', so exceptions in the handler are not isolated and bubble through _receive_loop.
At commit 568cbd1, BaseSession._receive_loop has no outer try/except around the async-for loop; request validation is also unguarded, so exceptions can abort the loop.

Fix commit 7b420656 wraps the whole receive loop in 'except anyio.ClosedResourceError' and 'except Exception' with logging to avoid crashing on unhandled exceptions, matching the vulnerability description.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability is an unhandled exception in the streamable HTTP transport leading to DoS, which was fixed by wrapping the `async for message in self._read_stream:` loop in a `try...except` block. The BIC merely refactored the message handling inside this loop (replacing stream writer sends with a method call) but did not introduce the loop itself nor remove any existing exception handling. Therefore, the BIC did not introduce the vulnerability.
evidence: The fix commit adds a try...except block around the `async for message in self._read_stream:` loop to catch `anyio.ClosedResourceError` and `Exception`.
The BIC refactored the message handling inside this loop, replacing `await self._incoming_message_stream_writer.send(...)` with `await self._handle_incoming(...)`.
The `async for message in self._read_stream:` loop already existed before the BIC and lacked the `try...except` block.

cves.description: MCP SDK Vulnerable to FastMCP Server Validation Error, Leading to Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: The application failed to properly handle or validate timeout configurations, potentially leading to resource exhaustion or unhandled exceptions when processing SSE streams.

bug_commits.author: David Soria Parra
bug_commits.message: Fix #201: Move incoming message stream from BaseSession to ServerSession (#325)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit replaced a robust memory object stream with direct calls to `_handle_incoming`, which lacks the error handling and timeout validation logic required for stable SSE stream processing. By removing the stream-based architecture, the commit introduced the vulnerability where unhandled exceptions or invalid timeout configurations during message processing can propagate and cause a denial of service.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to properly handle or validate timeout configurations, potentially leading to resource exhaustion or unhandled exceptions when processing SSE streams.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Inconsistent handling of timeout types (timedelta vs float) and lack of robust exception logging in SSE stream processing.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit removed the `_incoming_message_stream_writer` which acted as a buffer/queue for incoming messages. By switching to direct `_handle_incoming` calls, the session became susceptible to unhandled exceptions and resource exhaustion during message processing, as there was no longer a controlled stream mechanism to manage the flow and lifecycle of these requests.

bug_commits.pr_title: Fix #201: Move incoming message stream from BaseSession to ServerSession

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability is caused by missing exception handling around the `model_validate` call, which can raise validation errors and lead to a Denial of Service. The fix commit adds a try-except block around this call. The suspected bug-introducing commit (BIC) merely refactored the message stream handling and did not introduce the `model_validate` call itself, as it is visible in the context lines of the BIC diff. Therefore, the BIC did not introduce the vulnerability.
evidence: The fix commit adds a try-except block around `self._receive_request_type.model_validate` to catch validation errors.
The BIC diff shows that `self._receive_request_type.model_validate` was already present in the code before the BIC was applied (it appears in the context lines).

The BIC only refactored how messages are passed (replacing `_incoming_message_stream_writer.send` with direct calls to `_handle_incoming`), but did not introduce the `model_validate` call or remove any existing exception handling around it.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The later fix specifically wraps request-side `model_validate(...)` in `_receive_loop` with `try/except` and sends a JSON-RPC `INVALID_PARAMS` error instead of letting the exception escape. In the BIC, this same request-validation path remained unguarded while the commit refactored delivery from a buffered incoming-message stream to direct `_handle_incoming(...)` calls, so an invalid request would still raise out of `_receive_loop` and terminate processing. The BIC therefore preserved and reintroduced the exact vulnerable pattern in the affected code path that the fix later corrected.
evidence: At BIC 568cbd1a, `_receive_loop` processes `JSONRPCRequest` via `self._receive_request_type.model_validate(...)` with no surrounding `try/except`.
Fix commit 29c69e6a adds `try/except Exception as e:` around that exact request validation block and explicitly comments it prevents 'crashing the server'.

BIC changed the same `_receive_loop` logic in `src/mcp/shared/session.py`, replacing queued dispatch with direct `_handle_incoming(...)`, demonstrating it modified the vulnerable area rather than being merely adjacent.
Notification validation already had exception handling in the BIC, while request validation did not; the fix closes precisely that gap.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability (CVE-2025-53366) is about missing exception handling around `model_validate` for incoming JSONRPCRequest messages in `_receive_loop()`, which causes an uncaught exception that crashes the server (DoS). The fix adds a try/except block around the request validation code. The BIC (568cbd1a) only refactored the message dispatch mechanism from memory object streams (`_incoming_message_stream_writer.send()`) to a virtual method (`_handle_incoming()`). It did NOT touch the request validation code or add/remove any exception handling. The unprotected `model_validate` call for requests existed before the BIC and continued to exist after it. The BIC touched the same code region but did not introduce the specific vulnerable pattern.
evidence: The BIC diff shows no changes to the model_validate call for JSONRPCRequest or its surrounding exception handling
Before the BIC, the request validation code was already unprotected by try/except (visible in the diff context lines 307-322)
After the BIC, the same unprotected model_validate call remains at the same location
The BIC only changed dispatch: replacing _incoming_message_stream_writer.send() with _handle_incoming() calls
The notification branch already had try/except around model_validate both before and after the BIC, showing the request branch's missing try/except was a pre-existing omission

cves.description: AutoGPT Platform Exposes Graph Execution Results via Authorization Gap
cves.how_introduced: The application failed to properly validate user authorization when retrieving graph execution results, allowing unauthorized access to sensitive data.

bug_commits.author: Zamil Majdy
bug_commits.message: feat(backend): improve stop graph execution reliability (#10293)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `stop_graph_runs` endpoint and the underlying `_stop_graph_run` helper function. This implementation failed to properly validate authorization for the retrieved graph execution results, which was the specific pattern identified as the root cause of the vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to properly validate user authorization when retrieving graph execution results, allowing unauthorized access to sensitive data.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Returning raw response text without proper authorization checks or structured validation of the underlying API response.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced the `_stop_graph_run` function which retrieves graph executions based on user input without sufficient validation of ownership or authorization, leading to the exposure of sensitive execution results.

bug_commits.pr_title: feat(backend): improve stop graph execution reliability

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix commit resolves the vulnerability by removing the `stop_graph_runs` endpoint mapped to `/executions`. The provided BIC diff clearly shows that it is the commit that originally introduced this exact endpoint. Therefore, the BIC is the commit that introduced the vulnerability.
evidence: The fix commit removes the `stop_graph_runs` endpoint at `/executions`.
The BIC diff shows that it introduced the exact `stop_graph_runs` endpoint at `/executions` that was later removed in the fix commit.

Fix commit 309114a removed only this POST /executions endpoint from v1.py, indicating it was the vulnerable code path addressed by the security fix.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC (f1cc2afbda539ffe6beb01f7465227b3be3f9b49) introduced the `stop_graph_runs` endpoint at `POST /executions` in `autogpt_platform/backend/backend/server/routers/v1.py`. This endpoint did not exist before the BIC, as confirmed by searching for `stop_graph_runs` at the parent commit which returned no matches. The fix commit (309114a727baa2063357810d444e9a119f8dd7f6) specifically removes this exact endpoint from the blamed file. The CVE describes an "Authorization Gap" that exposes graph execution results, and this newly introduced endpoint returns `list[execution_db.GraphExecutionMeta]` execution data. The BIC is the direct and sole origin of the vulnerable code that was removed by the fix.

The fix commit removes exactly the `stop_graph_runs` endpoint code that was added by the BIC in the blamed file `autogpt_platform/backend/backend/server/routers/v1.py`

cves.description: Roo Code: Potential Remote Code Execution via .code-workspace
cves.how_introduced: The application failed to restrict access to sensitive .code-workspace files, allowing malicious workspace configurations to execute arbitrary commands.

bug_commits.author: Matt Rubens
bug_commits.message: Add a UI for managing slash commands (#6286)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `SlashCommandItem` component which allowed users to edit or delete any command, including built-in ones, by interacting with the UI. The fix commit reveals that the lack of a check for 'built-in' commands was the vulnerability, as it allowed potentially malicious workspace-defined commands to override or mimic system behavior.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to restrict access to sensitive .code-workspace files, allowing malicious workspace configurations to execute arbitrary commands.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of protection/validation for .code-workspace files allowing arbitrary command execution
llm_verdict.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit created the SlashCommandItem component. 2. It implemented handleEdit and handleDelete functions without checking the command source. 3. This allowed the UI to expose editing/deletion capabilities for all commands, including those that should be restricted (built-in), leading to the RCE vector via workspace configuration.

bug_commits.pr_title: Add a UI for managing slash commands

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix explicitly prevents edit/delete actions for built-in commands by gating the buttons on `command.source === "built-in"`, which indicates the vulnerable behavior was that built-in commands were previously editable/deletable through this UI. The suspected commit introduced `SlashCommandItem.tsx` with unconditional Edit and Delete buttons and a `handleEdit` path that posts `openFile` using `command.filePath`, making any command rendered there—including built-ins once exposed by the backend—actionable from the webview. This is the commit that added the vulnerable UI surface later corrected by the fix.
evidence: In BIC `webview-ui/src/components/chat/SlashCommandItem.tsx`, Edit/Delete buttons are always rendered for every command with no `source` check.
The fix commit adds `const isBuiltIn = command.source === "built-in"` and only shows action buttons when `!isBuiltIn`, directly targeting the vulnerable condition.
BIC also added `handleEdit` that sends `openFile` with `command.filePath`, enabling direct opening of command files from the webview.

The same feature commit updated backend messaging to include `filePath` in command data and added `openCommandFile`/`deleteCommand` handling, showing this commit introduced the command-management functionality that the fix later restricted.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2025-58372) is about "Potential Remote Code Execution via .code-workspace" which relates to the ability to load and execute slash commands from project directories that could be controlled by a malicious workspace file. The BIC (785fcac) only added a UI for managing slash commands (edit/delete/create buttons in SlashCommandItem.tsx), but the underlying command loading system that reads commands from project directories (src/services/command/commands.ts) already existed before this commit. The fix commit (2c82f4c) introduces "built-in" commands and prevents them from being edited/deleted in the UI, but this is primarily a new feature protection rather than fixing a vulnerability introduced by the BIC. The core vulnerability - loading arbitrary commands from workspace-controlled directories - predates the BIC.

bug_commits.author: Andras Bacsai
bug_commits.message: fix: add bash control structure keywords to sudo command processing

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced a flawed allow-list approach using `startsWith` to determine which commands should not be prefixed with `sudo`. Because `startsWith` does not enforce word boundaries, it allowed attackers to bypass the sudo prefixing by crafting commands that start with these keywords (e.g., 'docker' starts with 'do'), leading to command injection when the system executed these unsanitized strings.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to sanitize user-provided names used in proxy configuration files, allowing an authenticated attacker to inject malicious shell commands that are executed by the system during proxy startup.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: passing unsanitized user-controlled input (names) into shell commands or configuration files that are subsequently processed by shell execution
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added keywords like 'do' to the allow-list using `startsWith`. An attacker could provide a command like 'docker run...', which starts with 'do', causing the function to return the command without a 'sudo' prefix, but the underlying logic failed to properly isolate the command, allowing the injection of arbitrary shell commands that were then executed by the system.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected BIC did modify the exact helper later patched, but it added more excluded keywords to the naive startsWith()-based allowlist; it did not introduce the vulnerable pattern itself. The security fix addresses prefix-collision behavior in that preexisting logic (e.g. 'do' matching 'docker', 'if' matching 'ifconfig', 'fi' matching 'find') by replacing startsWith() with word-boundary regex checks. The BIC's purpose and tests were about avoiding bash syntax errors in control structures, and the same flawed startsWith() mechanism already existed before this commit for keywords like 'cd', 'command', 'if', and 'fi'.

The fix commit d9ec105... explicitly replaces startsWith() with regex word-boundary matching and notes collision examples such as 'do' vs 'docker', 'if' vs 'ifconfig', and 'fi' vs 'find'.
The BIC's added tests document the resulting non-security bug: 'docker' starts with 'do' and therefore does not get sudo prefix — described as a 'known limitation of the startsWith approach'.
Because the vulnerable matching strategy predated the BIC, 01635e8... expanded the impact surface but did not clearly introduce the underlying command-classification flaw that was later fixed.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability is an Authenticated Remote Code Execution via Command Injection in Database Backup, which was caused by failing to properly escape database names before using them in shell commands. The suspected bug-introducing commit (BIC) modified `bootstrap/helpers/sudo.php` to add bash control structures (like `do`, `done`, `for`) to a list of keywords that should not be prefixed with `sudo`. Because it used a simple `startsWith` check without word boundaries, it inadvertently caused commands like `docker` to lose their `sudo` prefix. While this was a bug that was fixed in the same release commit as the command injection, it is completely unrelated to the command injection vulnerability itself.

Because the BIC used startsWith without word boundaries, it inadvertently caused commands starting with 'do' (like 'docker') to not receive the sudo prefix.
This sudo prefix bug is completely unrelated to the command injection vulnerability in the database backup feature.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC added 'do' to the `startsWith` exclusion list in `parseCommandsByLineForSudo`, which causes `docker` commands to match the 'do' prefix and skip `sudo` prepending. This is the most critical prefix collision in Coolify (a Docker management platform), as docker commands are used extensively including in database backup operations. The fix specifically addresses this by replacing `startsWith` with word-boundary regex matching (`preg_match` with `(\s|;|$)` after each keyword), and explicitly calls out the 'do' vs 'docker' collision in its comments. While the `startsWith` pattern existed before with 'if' and 'fi', the BIC's addition of 'do' created the most exploitable prefix collision that directly impacts the database backup command injection vulnerability described in CVE-2025-66209.

cves.description: A flaw has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This affects the function mrb_vm_exec of the file src/vm.c of the component JMPNOT-to-JMPIF Optimization. Executing a manipulation can lead to use after free. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been published and may be used. This patch is called e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: The compiler optimization incorrectly assumed that a jump instruction was always a JMPNOT, leading to the modification of an arbitrary instruction opcode which could result in memory corruption or use-after-free during VM execution.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize JMPNOT+JMP to JMPIF in pattern matching

By adding the code block that unconditionally sets s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF without verifying the instruction type, it created the vulnerability that the fix commit later addressed by adding the missing check.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The compiler optimization incorrectly assumed that a jump instruction was always a JMPNOT, leading to the modification of an arbitrary instruction opcode which could result in memory corruption or use-after-free during VM execution.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF; // Unconditional modification of instruction opcode without verifying the original instruction type
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit implemented an optimization that assumes the instruction at fail_pos - 2 is always a JMPNOT. Because it lacked a check to verify this assumption, it allowed the compiler to overwrite arbitrary instructions with OP_JMPIF, leading to memory corruption during VM execution.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix commit addresses a vulnerability in the JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization by adding a check to ensure the instruction being modified is actually OP_JMPNOT. The BIC introduced this exact optimization logic, including the assumption that the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is OP_JMPNOT, without verifying it. Therefore, the BIC directly introduced the vulnerability.

The BIC introduces the exact JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization logic without checking if the instruction is actually OP_JMPNOT.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC (2b72d8a7) introduced the JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization in pattern matching code generation. This optimization blindly assumes that `fail_pos - 2` points to a JMPNOT instruction and overwrites it with OP_JMPIF, but it only checks two conditions: (1) single entry in the fail_pos chain, and (2) JMPNOT is immediately before current position. The fix (e50f15c1) adds a critical third condition — `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT` — to verify the instruction is actually JMPNOT before modifying it. Without this check, when a pinned variable is undefined, NODE_PAT_PIN generates a 3-byte OP_JMP instead of a 4-byte JMPNOT, causing `fail_pos - 2` to point into the previous instruction and corrupt its operand, leading to use-after-free during VM execution. This entire vulnerable optimization did not exist before the BIC.

cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: The limiter incorrectly conflated application-level limit exhaustion with DynamoDB WCU exhaustion, causing it to fail to re-shard or retry correctly, which allowed a single entity to trigger hot partition throttling.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added the `_is_wcu_exhausted` check to determine if shard doubling should occur, and a fallback to retry on another shard. Because this check was fragile and implicitly conflated application limits with WCU limits, it allowed a single entity to trigger hot partition throttling, leading to the Denial of Service vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced the shard doubling and shard retry logic. The fix commit modifies this exact logic to use `SpeculativeFailureReason` instead of `_is_wcu_exhausted` and adds a condition to only retry on other shards if the failure reason is `APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`. The BIC introduced the vulnerable pattern where shard retry happens regardless of the failure reason, which likely caused the DoS vulnerability.

The fix commit modifies the exact lines introduced by the BIC, changing the condition for shard retry to only happen if `result.failure_reason == SpeculativeFailureReason.APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`.
The BIC introduced the vulnerable pattern where shard retry happens regardless of the failure reason, which likely caused the DoS vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced the exact control flow that caused the vulnerability: on any speculative failure with `result.shard_count > 1`, the limiter retries another shard, while WCU exhaustion was detected only by scanning `old_buckets` for a low `wcu` token count. That heuristic cannot distinguish pure WCU exhaustion from simultaneous application-limit exhaustion, so the new retry logic can redirect requests to other shards even when a target shard is throttled at the infrastructure layer, enabling concentrated per-entity hot-partition abuse instead of shard doubling. The fix adds an explicit `failure_reason` classification in the repository and restricts retry to `APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED` only, confirming the BIC’s retry-on-ambiguous-failure logic introduced the flaw.
evidence: BIC 9f66c42 added `_is_wcu_exhausted(result.old_buckets)` plus unconditional `if result.shard_count > 1: _retry_on_other_shard(...)` in `limiter.py`.
At the BIC, `SpeculativeResult` in `repository_protocol.py` had no `failure_reason`; limiter decisions were based only on `old_buckets`, `shard_id`, and `shard_count`.
In repository code at the BIC, failed speculative writes returned only `old_buckets` and `shard_count`, so limiter could not tell whether failure was WCU-only, app-limit-only, or both.

Fix commit 481ce44 introduces `SpeculativeFailureReason` with `APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`, `WCU_EXHAUSTED`, and `BOTH_EXHAUSTED`, and changes limiter logic to double shards on WCU/BOTH but retry other shards only on APP-only exhaustion.

Fix repository code explicitly classifies failure as `BOTH_EXHAUSTED` when both low WCU and low app tokens are present, proving the prior BIC logic misclassified this case and took the wrong retry path.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced both vulnerable code patterns that the fix addresses: (1) the `_is_wcu_exhausted()` heuristic that inspects `old_buckets` token levels to detect WCU exhaustion, which was replaced by a deterministic `SpeculativeFailureReason` enum check, and (2) the unconditional shard retry `if result.shard_count > 1:` that retries on ANY multi-shard failure regardless of reason, which was fixed to only retry when `failure_reason == APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`. The unconditional retry meant that when WCU exhaustion wasn't properly detected by the heuristic, the code would retry on other shards instead of doubling the shard count, failing to distribute writes across DynamoDB partitions and enabling per-entity DoS through hot partition throttling. Neither of these code patterns existed before the BIC.

llm_verdict.vuln_description: The limiter incorrectly conflated application-level limit exhaustion with DynamoDB WCU exhaustion, causing it to fail to re-shard or retry correctly, which allowed a single entity to trigger hot partition throttling.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Implicit and fragile failure classification using _is_wcu_exhausted() to distinguish between app-limit exhaustion and WCU exhaustion
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented proactive sharding based on `wcu_tc_delta` (consumption rate), which failed to accurately reflect the actual token balance, leading the system to ignore hot partition throttling until it was too late.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability is a Denial of Service caused by DynamoDB hot partition throttling, which occurs because the proactive sharding logic fails to trigger correctly. The fix commit changes the sharding trigger from checking the consumption in a single batch (`wcu_tc_delta`) to checking the remaining token level (`wcu_tk_milli`). The BIC introduced the `try_proactive_shard` function and the exact flawed logic based on `wcu_tc_delta` that was later fixed, confirming it as the commit that introduced the vulnerability.
evidence: The fix commit changes `try_proactive_shard` to use `wcu_tk_milli` (remaining tokens) instead of `wcu_tc_delta` (consumption in the current batch) to trigger proactive sharding.
The BIC (6bcd260679f3838743cad07da62628487267e914) introduces the `try_proactive_shard` function and the exact flawed logic that uses `wcu_tc_delta` and `WCU_PROACTIVE_THRESHOLD = 0.8`.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC directly introduced the vulnerable proactive-sharding logic and based its trigger on per-batch WCU consumption delta (`wcu_info.tc_delta / cp_milli >= 0.8`) rather than remaining token level or actual partition pressure. The later fix changes exactly this decision variable to `tk_milli` and updates the threshold semantics to shard when tokens are low, indicating the original algorithm was the bug. Because proactive sharding is the mechanism intended to prevent DynamoDB hot partitions, this incorrect trigger could fail to split overloaded per-entity buckets under sustained write pressure, enabling the described per-entity DoS.
evidence: BIC 6bcd260 adds `try_proactive_shard()` and wires it into `process_stream_records()` for the first time.
In the BIC, `try_proactive_shard()` uses `wcu_tc_delta` (accumulated batch consumption delta) and shards only when `consumption_ratio = wcu_tc_delta / wcu_capacity_milli` exceeds 0.8.

Fix commit 481ce44 changes the same call site and function to use `wcu_tk_milli` (remaining tokens from `NewImage`) and shard when token ratio falls below 0.2, explicitly reframing the logic from batch consumption to low token level.

The vulnerability description is about DynamoDB hot partition throttling causing per-entity DoS; the BIC is the commit that introduced the flawed protective sharding heuristic meant to mitigate that exact condition.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC (6bcd260) introduced the `try_proactive_shard` function with a flawed proactive sharding signal: it used `wcu_tc_delta` (consumption delta within a batch) compared against an 80% threshold (`consumption_ratio = wcu_tc_delta / wcu_capacity_milli`). This metric was unreliable because `tc_delta` only captures consumption within a single Lambda batch (max 100 records), making the 80% threshold effectively unreachable under normal conditions. This meant DynamoDB hot partitions would never trigger proactive sharding, enabling per-entity Denial of Service via hot partition throttling (CWE-770). The fix commit (481ce44) corrected this by switching to `wcu_tk_milli` (remaining token level from the last NewImage) with a 20% low-water-mark threshold, which reliably detects sustained write pressure regardless of batch size.
evidence: The BIC introduced the entire `try_proactive_shard` function with the vulnerable consumption-ratio-based threshold: `consumption_ratio = wcu_tc_delta / wcu_capacity_milli` compared against `WCU_PROACTIVE_THRESHOLD = 0.8`

The fix commit changed the signal from `wcu_tc_delta` (batch consumption delta) to `wcu_tk_milli` (remaining token level) and inverted the threshold logic: `token_ratio = wcu_tk_milli / wcu_capacity_milli; if token_ratio >= WCU_PROACTIVE_THRESHOLD_LOW (0.2): return False`

The BIC's own design review (Task 34 in the fix commit's plan doc) explicitly acknowledges the batch size boundary problem: 'at BatchSize=100, the maximum achievable delta is 100 * 1000 = 100,000 milli... the threshold is unreachable at the current batch size'
The function did not exist before the BIC - it was entirely new code added in this commit, as confirmed by the diff showing all `+` lines for the function definition
The caller in `process_stream_records` was also added in the BIC, passing `wcu_tc_delta=wcu_info.tc_delta` which the fix changed to `wcu_tk_milli=wcu_info.tk_milli`

cves.description: MCP Go SDK Vulnerable to Improper Handling of Case Sensitivity
cves.how_introduced: The SDK used the default Go JSON unmarshaler which performs case-insensitive field matching. This can lead to security bypasses or logic errors when multiple JSON fields differ only by case, or when strict protocol compliance is required.

bug_commits.author: Robert Findley
bug_commits.message: mcp: implement sampling with tools (#699)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `ToolResultContent.MarshalJSON` method which contains the vulnerable `json.Unmarshal(data, &w)` call using the standard library's case-insensitive unmarshaler. This specific code block was directly targeted and replaced by the fix commit to enforce strict case sensitivity.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The SDK used the default Go JSON unmarshaler which performs case-insensitive field matching. This can lead to security bypasses or logic errors when multiple JSON fields differ only by case, or when strict protocol compliance is required.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: json.Unmarshal(data, v) // using standard library encoding/json
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added the ToolResultContent struct and its MarshalJSON implementation -> This implementation used the standard library's json.Unmarshal to process nested content -> The standard library's unmarshaler is case-insensitive by default, creating the vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability is caused by improper handling of case sensitivity during JSON unmarshaling, as the standard `encoding/json` package is case-insensitive for struct fields. The fix commit addresses this by replacing `json.Unmarshal` with a custom `internaljson.Unmarshal` that enforces case sensitivity. The suspected bug-introducing commit (BIC) directly introduced the `ToolResultContent.MarshalJSON` and `unmarshalContent` functions in `mcp/content.go`, both of which use the vulnerable `json.Unmarshal` function. Therefore, the BIC is confirmed to have introduced the vulnerability.
evidence: The fix commit replaces `json.Unmarshal` with `internaljson.Unmarshal` to enforce case sensitivity.
The BIC introduced `ToolResultContent.MarshalJSON` and `unmarshalContent` in `mcp/content.go`.
Both of these functions introduced by the BIC use `json.Unmarshal`, which is the exact vulnerable pattern that was later fixed.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced the exact vulnerable parsing pattern in mcp/content.go when it added ToolUseContent/ToolResultContent support. Specifically, it newly added json.Unmarshal calls for nested content conversion in ToolResultContent.MarshalJSON and for parsing raw content in unmarshalContent; the later fix changes those exact call sites to internaljson.Unmarshal, whose decoder explicitly disables case-insensitive struct-field matching. Since the CVE is about improper handling of case sensitivity, and these vulnerable call sites did not exist before this commit, the BIC is the origin of the flaw.
evidence: BIC 3cf9f990 added ToolResultContent.MarshalJSON with `json.Unmarshal(data, &w)` and added `unmarshalContent` using `json.Unmarshal(raw, &wires)` / `json.Unmarshal(raw, &wire)` in mcp/content.go.
Fix 7b8d81c changes precisely those three call sites from `encoding/json.Unmarshal` to `internaljson.Unmarshal`.
At the fix commit, internal/json/json.go defines Unmarshal using segmentio/json decoder with `DontMatchCaseInsensitiveStructFields()`, directly addressing case-sensitivity handling.
Search at the BIC found no prior use of `internaljson.Unmarshal`, indicating the new sampling/tools content path was introduced with ordinary case-insensitive decoding.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced all three `json.Unmarshal` calls in `mcp/content.go` that the fix commit later changed to case-sensitive `internaljson.Unmarshal`. Before the BIC, there were zero `json.Unmarshal` calls in this file. The BIC added: (1) `json.Unmarshal` in `ToolResultContent.MarshalJSON()`, and (2) the entire `unmarshalContent()` function containing two `json.Unmarshal` calls. These case-insensitive unmarshal calls are the exact vulnerability (CWE-178: Improper Handling of Case Sensitivity) that CVE-2026-27896 describes, as Go's standard `encoding/json` performs case-insensitive field matching, potentially allowing attackers to bypass content type validation by using differently-cased JSON field names like "Type" instead of "type".
evidence: Before the BIC (parent commit cc30d2a), there were no json.Unmarshal calls in mcp/content.go
The BIC introduced ToolResultContent.MarshalJSON() containing json.Unmarshal(data, &w) - later fixed at line 251
The BIC introduced the unmarshalContent() function with two json.Unmarshal calls (lines 331, 335) - both later fixed
The fix commit replaced all three json.Unmarshal calls with internaljson.Unmarshal which uses DontMatchCaseInsensitiveStructFields()
The fix commit message explicitly states: 'case insensitive unmarshaling can pose security issues'

cves.description: OpenClaw Twitch allowFrom is not enforced in optional plugin, unauthorized chat users can trigger agent pipeline
cves.how_introduced: The Twitch plugin failed to enforce the 'allowFrom' allowlist, causing the system to fall back to open access and allowing unauthorized users to trigger agent pipelines.

bug_commits.author: jaydenfyi
bug_commits.message: feat: Twitch Plugin (#1612)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the initial test suite for access control, which explicitly codified the incorrect behavior where requests fell back to 'allowed: true' even when 'allowFrom' was configured. By establishing these tests as the baseline, the commit solidified the flawed logic that the fix commit later had to reverse.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The Twitch plugin failed to enforce the 'allowFrom' allowlist, causing the system to fall back to open access and allowing unauthorized users to trigger agent pipelines.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: The access control logic treated the 'allowFrom' configuration as optional or non-exclusive, allowing requests to proceed even when the sender was not in the defined allowlist.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit created test cases that asserted 'allowed: true' for users not in the 'allowFrom' list, effectively defining the vulnerable 'open access' fallback as the expected behavior in the codebase.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit introduces the Twitch plugin and its access control logic. In the `checkTwitchAccessControl` function, the `allowFrom` check only returns true if the user is in the allowlist, but fails to return false if they are not. This causes the logic to fall through, ultimately returning true if no roles are configured, which completely bypasses the intended allowlist restriction. The fix commit directly addresses this by adding the missing return statement to block unauthorized users.
evidence: The BIC `f5c90f0e5c7a12285ceea6c3102666a7b904b16f` introduces the Twitch plugin and the `checkTwitchAccessControl` function in `extensions/twitch/src/access-control.ts`.
In `checkTwitchAccessControl`, if `account.allowFrom` is set but the sender is not in the allowlist, the function falls through to the next check instead of returning `{ allowed: false }`.
If `account.allowedRoles` is not set, the function ultimately returns `{ allowed: true }` at the end, allowing unauthorized users to bypass the `allowFrom` restriction.

The fix commit `8c7901c984866a776eb59662dc9d8b028de4f0d0` corrects this exact logic by returning `{ allowed: false, reason: "sender is not in allowFrom allowlist" }` when the sender is not in the `allowFrom` list.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected BIC is the initial introduction of the Twitch plugin, and it introduced the vulnerable logic in extensions/twitch/src/access-control.ts: when allowFrom is configured and the sender is not on that allowlist, the function does not deny access and instead falls through to allowedRoles or the final allowed:true fallback. The later fix adds an explicit deny return for non-allowlisted senders and updates tests from expecting open access/role bypass to expecting denial when allowFrom is set. Since monitor.ts invokes checkTwitchAccessControl before dispatching inbound chat messages into the agent pipeline, this flawed fallthrough directly enabled unauthorized users to trigger processing.
evidence: At BIC f5c90f0e5c7a12285ceea6c3102666a7b904b16f, access-control.ts contains: if allowFrom includes senderId => allow, but otherwise no deny; execution continues to allowedRoles/final allowed:true.

At fix commit 8c7901c984866a776eb59662dc9d8b028de4f0d0, access-control.ts adds explicit return { allowed:false, reason:'sender is not in allowFrom allowlist' } immediately after the allowFrom check fails.

The fix also changes tests from 'allows users not in allowlist via fallback (open access)' and 'allows user with role even if not in allowlist' to blocking behavior, proving the prior behavior was considered incorrect and security-relevant.
monitor.ts at the BIC uses checkTwitchAccessControl and, when access.allowed is true, proceeds to processTwitchMessage(), establishing the causal path to unauthorized agent pipeline triggering.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC (f5c90f0e) introduced the entire Twitch plugin, including the vulnerable `access-control.ts` implementation. The vulnerability is that when `allowFrom` is configured, users NOT in the allowlist are not blocked — the code falls through to subsequent checks or the final `return { allowed: true }` fallback. The fix commit adds an explicit `return { allowed: false, reason: "sender is not in allowFrom allowlist" }` after the allowlist miss. The BIC's test file even explicitly validated this wrong behavior with a test named "allows users not in allowlist via fallback (open access)" — the exact test that the fix changes to "blocks users not in allowlist when allowFrom is set".
evidence: The BIC introduced access-control.ts with the vulnerable pattern: when allowFrom is set and user is not in the list, code falls through instead of returning allowed:false
The fix adds `return { allowed: false, reason: 'sender is not in allowFrom allowlist' }` after the allowlist check fails in access-control.ts
The BIC's test explicitly validated the wrong behavior: 'allows users not in allowlist via fallback (open access)' with expect(result.allowed).toBe(true)
The fix changes that test to 'blocks users not in allowlist when allowFrom is set' with expect(result.allowed).toBe(false)
No prior version of access-control.ts existed — the BIC created the entire file

cves.description: OpenClaw: denial of service through large base64 media files allocating large buffers before limit checks
cves.how_introduced: The application attempted to decode base64-encoded media attachments into memory buffers before validating their size, allowing an attacker to trigger large memory allocations and crash the process.

bug_commits.author: cristip73
bug_commits.message: fix: enable image attachments in chat messages for Claude API

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the logic that performs `Buffer.from(b64, 'base64').byteLength` to calculate the size of the attachment. This implementation forces the Node.js runtime to decode the entire base64 string into a buffer in memory before the size limit check is performed, which is the root cause of the resource exhaustion vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application attempted to decode base64-encoded media attachments into memory buffers before validating their size, allowing an attacker to trigger large memory allocations and crash the process.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Buffer.from(userInput, 'base64') without prior validation of the input string length
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit added the `parseMessageWithAttachments` function, which uses `Buffer.from(b64, 'base64')` to determine the size of user-provided base64 strings. Because this operation allocates memory for the decoded data before validating the size against `maxBytes`, an attacker can provide an arbitrarily large base64 string to trigger a crash.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit c4e76eb6355fced2b1715d39c55fdf01b5715a4e introduced the new parseMessageWithAttachments() path that decodes attacker-controlled base64 via Buffer.from(b64, "base64").byteLength before enforcing maxBytes. That is exactly the vulnerable pattern described in the CVE: a large base64 media payload can force allocation of a large decoded buffer before the size-limit check runs, enabling DoS. The later fix removes this eager decode in both parseMessageWithAttachments() and buildMessageWithAttachments() and replaces it with estimateBase64DecodedBytes(), explicitly to avoid full decode allocations for large payloads.
evidence: BIC adds parseMessageWithAttachments() in src/gateway/chat-attachments.ts with: sizeBytes = Buffer.from(b64, "base64").byteLength; followed only afterward by if (sizeBytes <= 0 || sizeBytes > maxBytes) throw ...
Commit metadata for BIC says it 'enable[s] image attachments in chat messages for Claude API', so this newly introduced path became the feature handling user-supplied image/base64 attachments.

Fix commit 31791233d60495725fa012745dde8d6ee69e9595 replaces Buffer.from(..., "base64").byteLength with estimateBase64DecodedBytes() and adds comment 'avoid full decode allocations for large payloads.'
The helper introduced by the fix, src/media/base64.ts, explicitly states it exists to enforce budgets before Buffer.from(..., "base64"), confirming the causal mechanism.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit added a new function `parseMessageWithAttachments` which contains the vulnerable pattern of allocating a buffer to check base64 length. However, this exact same vulnerable pattern was already present in the existing `buildMessageWithAttachments` function, which the commit merely deprecated and copied from. Therefore, the commit did not introduce the vulnerability, as it already existed in the codebase.
evidence: The BIC added `parseMessageWithAttachments` which contains the vulnerable `Buffer.from(b64, "base64").byteLength` code.
However, the exact same vulnerable code was already present in the existing `buildMessageWithAttachments` function.
The BIC merely copied the existing vulnerable pattern to a new function, but did not introduce the vulnerability to the codebase.

cves.how_introduced: The library failed to hook all deserialization entry points (specifically pickle.loads and _pickle.loads), allowing attackers to bypass safety checks by using these unhooked functions.

bug_commits.author: Lucas Bourtoule
bug_commits.message: Fix PyTorch v1.3+ hook bypass by hooking pickle.Unpickler class

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `run_hook()` function which only hooked `pickle.load` while failing to hook `pickle.loads` and `_pickle.loads`. Although the commit included `always_check_safety()` which did hook those functions, the primary `run_hook()` entry point remained incomplete, creating the bypass vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The library failed to hook all deserialization entry points (specifically pickle.loads and _pickle.loads), allowing attackers to bypass safety checks by using these unhooked functions.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: hooking only pickle.load while leaving pickle.loads and _pickle.loads unmonitored
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit implemented `run_hook()` with an incomplete set of monkey-patches, specifically omitting `pickle.loads` and `_pickle.loads`, which allowed users calling the standard `run_hook()` to remain vulnerable to deserialization attacks via those unmonitored functions.

bug_commits.pr_title: Fix PyTorch v1.3+ hook bypass by hooking pickle.Unpickler class

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC added a new `run_hook()`/`always_check_safety()` implementation that hooked only `pickle.load` and `pickle.Unpickler`/`_pickle.Unpickler`, but left `pickle.loads` and `_pickle.loads` untouched. The later fix commit specifically remedied this exact omission by adding hooks for `pickle.loads` and `_pickle.loads` in `run_hook()`. Because `always_check_safety()` is just an alias for `run_hook()`, the BIC directly created the bypass condition described in the advisory.
evidence: At BIC commit 8714627, `fickling/hook.py` shows `always_check_safety()` calls `run_hook()` and `run_hook()` only assigns `pickle.load = loader.load` plus Unpickler hooks; it does not hook `pickle.loads` or `_pickle.loads`.

The fix commit 8c24c6e modifies the same `run_hook()` to add `_pickle.load = loader.load`, `pickle.loads = loader.loads`, and `_pickle.loads = loader.loads`, indicating these missing hooks were the vulnerability fix.

Repository search at the fixed commit shows tests/examples and code paths using `pickle.loads`, consistent with an exploitable bypass when `always_check_safety()` failed to replace those entry points.

cves.description: OpenClaw's typed sender-key matching for toolsBySender prevents identity-collision policy bypass
cves.how_introduced: The application allowed ambiguous sender identification in `toolsBySender` configurations, where mutable fields like display names could collide with unique identifiers, allowing unauthorized users to impersonate others and gain elevated tool access.

bug_commits.author: adam91holt
bug_commits.message: Add per-sender group tool policies and fix precedence (#1757)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the test cases that established the vulnerable pattern of using raw strings (e.g., 'alice', 'admin') as keys in the `toolsBySender` configuration. By codifying this pattern in the test suite, it solidified the expectation that the system should match senders without type prefixes, which directly enabled the identity-collision vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application allowed ambiguous sender identification in `toolsBySender` configurations, where mutable fields like display names could collide with unique identifiers, allowing unauthorized users to impersonate others and gain elevated tool access.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Matching sender authorization keys without explicit type prefixes (e.g., using raw strings that could be interpreted as either an ID or a mutable display name)
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The commit introduced test cases that defined `toolsBySender` keys as raw strings, forcing the implementation to treat these strings as ambiguous identifiers. This lack of type-prefixed keys allowed the system to match mutable display names against unique IDs, leading to the authorization bypass.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The suspected commit 3b0c80ce added the per-sender group tool policy feature and its matching logic in src/config/group-policy.ts. That logic normalized untyped keys and then matched them against senderId, senderE164, senderUsername, and senderName in sequence, meaning a policy intended for one identity string could be triggered by a different identity field with the same value. The later fix changed the core matching logic to require typed prefixes (id:, e164:, username:, name:) and explicitly documented/tests that untyped legacy keys map to senderId only, which directly addresses the identity-collision bypass introduced by the BIC.
evidence: BIC diff for src/config/group-policy.ts introduced resolveToolsBySender and resolveChannelGroupToolsPolicy with toolsBySender support.
At 3b0c80ce, resolveToolsBySender stored untyped normalized keys in one Map and matched candidates from senderId, senderE164, senderUsername, and senderName interchangeably.
Fix diff for src/config/group-policy.ts replaced this with typed sender-key parsing/buckets and warns that legacy unprefixed keys are matched as id only.
Fix-era tests in src/config/group-policy.test.ts specifically assert that senderName collisions must not match id keys and that untyped legacy keys are senderId-only.
The test-file fix only changed examples from alice/admin to id:alice/id:admin, consistent with correcting the vulnerable matching semantics rather than a mere test-only issue.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced the `resolveToolsBySender` function, which implemented the `toolsBySender` policy matching. The implementation checked the policy keys against multiple sender attributes (`senderId`, `senderE164`, `senderUsername`, `senderName`) without distinguishing between them. This allowed an attacker to bypass the policy by changing a user-controlled attribute like `senderName` to match a privileged `senderId`, causing the identity-collision vulnerability described in the CVE.
evidence: The BIC 3b0c80ce245b293fae817ec800c5e3a9c0ec7ee8 introduced the `resolveToolsBySender` function in `src/config/group-policy.ts`.
In the BIC, `resolveToolsBySender` normalizes the `toolsBySender` keys and checks them against a list of candidates: `senderId`, `senderE164`, `senderUsername`, and `senderName`.
Because all candidates are checked against the same untyped keys, a user could bypass the policy by setting their `senderName` or `senderUsername` to match a privileged `senderId` (e.g., "admin").
The fix commit 5547a2275cb69413af3b62c795b93214fe913b57 resolves this by introducing typed keys (e.g., "id:admin", "username:alice") and mapping legacy untyped keys strictly to `senderId`.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC (3b0c80ce) introduced the `toolsBySender` feature including the `resolveToolsBySender` function in `src/config/group-policy.ts`. This function used a single flat Map to store all sender policy keys and matched them against all sender attributes (senderId, senderE164, senderUsername, senderName) without distinguishing which attribute type a key was intended to match. This created an identity-collision vulnerability (CWE-863) where a config key like "alice" intended for a specific sender ID could be matched by any user whose username, display name, or phone number normalized to "alice", granting them unintended elevated tool permissions. The fix (5547a227) introduced typed sender-key prefixes (id:, e164:, username:, name:) with separate lookup buckets to prevent this cross-attribute collision. The test changes in the fix directly modified the test cases introduced by the BIC, changing `alice` to `id:alice` and `admin` to `id:admin`.
evidence: BIC introduced resolveToolsBySender() with a single flat Map matching all sender attributes (senderId, senderE164, senderUsername, senderName) against the same untyped keys
BIC added test cases using untyped keys 'alice' and 'admin' in toolsBySender config
Fix commit changed these exact test keys to 'id:alice' and 'id:admin' and introduced typed sender-key buckets (id, e164, username, name) to prevent cross-attribute collision
The vulnerable normalizeSenderKey function stripped @ prefixes and lowercased all keys uniformly, making username/name/id collisions trivial

cves.description: OpenClaw has unbounded memory growth in Zalo webhook via query-string key churn (unauthenticated DoS)
cves.how_introduced: The application failed to bound the tracking state for webhook requests, allowing an attacker to trigger unbounded memory growth by sending requests with unique, arbitrary query-string keys.

bug_commits.author: Jean-Marc
bug_commits.message: feat(channels): add Synology Chat native channel (#23012)

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `RateLimiter` class, which uses an unbounded `Map<string, number[]>` to track request timestamps per user ID. Because this map is keyed by user-controlled input without a maximum size constraint, it allows an attacker to exhaust memory by flooding the system with unique keys, which is the exact root cause of the reported vulnerability.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to bound the tracking state for webhook requests, allowing an attacker to trigger unbounded memory growth by sending requests with unique, arbitrary query-string keys.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: storing request metadata or security keys in an unbounded map/object keyed by user-controlled query parameters
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The implementation of the `RateLimiter` class used a `Map` to store request history for every unique `userId` without enforcing a limit on the number of entries in the map, leading to unbounded memory growth when processing arbitrary query-string keys.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC added the vulnerable RateLimiter implementation from scratch in extensions/synology-chat/src/security.ts. That implementation stores per-user timestamp arrays in an unbounded Map and is used by the webhook handler keyed on attacker-controlled payload.user_id, with cleanup only occurring every 5 windows; this allows unauthenticated churn of many distinct IDs/keys to grow memory without an upper bound. The later fix directly replaces this exact logic with a bounded fixed-window limiter that enforces maxTrackedKeys, showing the original design was the root cause.
evidence: BIC 03586e3d introduced RateLimiter with `private requests: Map<string, number[]> = new Map();` and no cap on keys or entries.
In BIC security.ts, `check(userId)` creates/stores a fresh array for any new userId and writes it back into the Map, so arbitrary key churn expands memory.
At the same commit, webhook-handler.ts calls `rateLimiter.check(payload.user_id)` where `payload.user_id` comes from parsed webhook input, making the Map key attacker-controlled.
Fix commit 43cad826 replaces the custom Map-based limiter with `createFixedWindowRateLimiter(... maxTrackedKeys ...)`, explicitly bounding tracked users and eliminating the unbounded-memory pattern.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC introduced the custom `RateLimiter` class in `extensions/synology-chat/src/security.ts`. This implementation used a `Map` to track request timestamps per user ID but failed to limit the maximum number of tracked users. This allowed an attacker to cause unbounded memory growth by churning user IDs, which is the exact vulnerability described and fixed in the later commit.
evidence: The BIC 03586e3d0057b5975090d50dadcc5bc95b51f977 introduced the `RateLimiter` class in `extensions/synology-chat/src/security.ts`.
The `RateLimiter` class uses a `Map` to store timestamps per `userId` and only cleans up stale entries periodically, without limiting the number of tracked users.
This allows an attacker to cause unbounded memory growth by sending requests with many different `userId`s (e.g., via query-string key churn).

The fix commit 43cad8268d46a3663c79be831f2cacb015114882 replaces this custom implementation with `createFixedWindowRateLimiter` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk` which limits the number of tracked keys (`maxTrackedKeys`).

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC (commit 03586e3) created the file `extensions/synology-chat/src/security.ts` from scratch, introducing the `RateLimiter` class with an unbounded `Map<string, number[]>` for tracking requests per user ID. This Map has no upper bound on the number of tracked keys - the periodic cleanup only removes stale entries but never caps total entries. An attacker could cause unbounded memory growth by churning through unique user IDs or keys. The fix commit replaces this exact pattern with a bounded `createFixedWindowRateLimiter` that enforces `maxTrackedKeys: 5_000`, directly addressing the CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption) vulnerability in the code introduced by this BIC.
evidence: The BIC created extensions/synology-chat/src/security.ts with `private requests: Map<string, number[]> = new Map()` - an unbounded Map with no maxTrackedKeys limit
The cleanup method only removes entries with no recent timestamps but never caps the total number of entries in the Map
The fix commit replaces this unbounded Map with `createFixedWindowRateLimiter` that has `maxTrackedKeys: 5_000`
The BIC did not modify any Zalo code - the Zalo unbounded Maps existed before this commit, but the blamed file is specifically the Synology Chat security.ts which was entirely created by this BIC
The vulnerability pattern (CWE-400: unbounded memory growth) is directly present in the RateLimiter class introduced by this commit

cves.description: OpenClaw vulnerable to sensitive file disclosure via stageSandboxMedia
cves.how_introduced: The application failed to validate file paths when accessing attachments, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files on the system by providing malicious paths.

bug_commits.author: Tyler Yust
bug_commits.message: feat: native image injection for vision-capable models

llm_verdict.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the logic that processes and exposes attachment paths (mediaPaths) without any validation. This created the vulnerable pattern where arbitrary file paths provided in attachments were accepted and used by the application, which the fix commit subsequently addressed by adding path validation.
llm_verdict.vuln_description: The application failed to validate file paths when accessing attachments, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files on the system by providing malicious paths.
llm_verdict.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing file paths for attachments without validating them against a restricted set of allowed root directories.
llm_verdict.causal_chain: The blamed commit added the logic to extract and map multiple attachment paths into the application state without checking if those paths were restricted to allowed directories, directly enabling the path traversal vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability is a sensitive file disclosure caused by a lack of path validation on attachment paths. The fix introduces `isInboundPathAllowed` to validate these paths. The suspected BIC merely refactored the attachment extraction to support multiple attachments (changing `find` to `filter`), but the code prior to the BIC was already extracting and using `original_path` without any validation. Therefore, the BIC did not introduce the vulnerability; it only modified the lines that were later fixed.
evidence: The fix commit adds path validation (`isInboundPathAllowed`) to the attachment paths extracted from `original_path`.
The BIC diff shows that before the BIC, the code was already extracting `original_path` without any validation via `attachments?.find((entry) => entry?.original_path && !entry?.missing)`.
The BIC merely refactored the code to support multiple attachments by changing `find` to `filter`, but the underlying lack of path validation existed prior to the BIC.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The BIC directly changed iMessage attachment handling from selecting a single attachment to collecting all attachment original_path values into MediaPaths/MediaUrls without any path-root validation. Those fields are consumed by stageSandboxMedia, which copies local files or SCP-fetches remote files based on the supplied paths, so attacker-controlled original_path values could trigger sensitive file disclosure. The later fix in the same file adds explicit isInboundPathAllowed checks and allowed attachment roots exactly at the point where the BIC introduced the broader propagation of attachment paths.
evidence: BIC 8d74578 added validAttachments/map logic and populated MediaPaths and MediaUrls from attachment.original_path values in src/imessage/monitor/monitor-provider.ts.
At BIC, validAttachments only required entry?.original_path && !entry?.missing; there was no allowed-root or traversal/path policy check.
At BIC, src/auto-reply/reply/stage-sandbox-media.ts reads ctx.MediaPaths/MediaPath and fs.copyFile()s or scpFile()s each absolute path into the sandbox/remote cache.
Fix commit 1316e574 modifies the same iMessage attachment filtering code to call isInboundPathAllowed with resolved attachment roots and drop attachments outside allowed roots.
